goals.g1: Record the implementation and test results of the refined step 5 (exact preserved-id-set comparison, firing-count backstop) against both the synthetic regression and the real production case.

prep_note: Implements ers_step5_implementation.json's committed design. Prepared before recording.

prior_candidates.statement: Sven (direct, this session): step 5 as built only checks existence, not correspondence -- a useless implementation. Proposed alternative: record forward creations (born+survived nodes, tagged by which match/firing they came from), compare against backward findings for discrepancies. Since node identity is not given for born content, this likely requires a LOCAL anchored isomorphism check per match -- which raises whether step 9 (the current global isomorphism check) becomes redundant, and notes compound cases would need multiple tries to find the right pairing when identity isn't preserved (id-preserving/survived cases are trivial by contrast). Asks: is there any cheaper way to say 'this set of created nodes corresponds to this match'? Otherwise a simple rule-activation count is easier and still useful.
goals: Answer Sven's design question precisely: does pushing per-match discrepancy comparison (recorded forward creations/survivals vs backward findings) into step 5 make step 9's global isomorphism check redundant? Is there a cheap way to say 'this set of created nodes corresponds to this match' besides a local anchored isomorphism check? Determine whether id-preserving vs genuinely-born content require fundamentally different answers.

prior_candidates.statement: Implemented in basic_machinery/reverse_compound.py: build_reversed_operation (wraps build_reversed_graph2+compile_schema, cached), collect_reversed_matches (phase 1, blind match_all per rule, flags per-rule multiplicity), resolve_reverse_compound (phase 2, OV_forward vs OV_reverse structural comparison via detect_overlaps reused unmodified twice -- no ordering, no enumeration, per this session's correction). Verified end-to-end against a single-rule synthetic case using an explicit-category rule. NOT verified: any genuine multi-rule compound firing (no test built); footprint_is_consistent is still not wired to a real apply_compound aggregate; the full isomorphism-to-retained-previous-layer check is not implemented at all -- resolve_reverse_compound only gets as far as the OV comparison. A real, uncorrected defect was found and left uncorrected (root_cause_and_rule_misapplied): reversed rules built from a forward rule using ph_all_four (blanket, variable-presence preservation) will incorrectly find zero matches against real firings where fewer than all 4 declared categories are actually present, because the exact-degree matcher cannot express 'however many of these are present'. This affects any real rule using ph_all_four (at least spine_finalise_v1.py's out_rspine, confirmed earlier this session) and is carried forward, not fixed.

derived_from.rule: state plainly what is verified working, what is unverified, and what is a known, real, uncorrected defect -- not overclaiming completeness

goals: Implement the corrected reversibility check design (per ers_lf6_repair.json and ers_compose_reversed_compound.json's surviving structure): collect_reversed_matches(matches, result_graph) -- per-rule blind match_all of each reversed rule against the result, flagging per-rule multiplicity; resolve_reverse_compound -- compare OV_forward (detect_overlaps on the known forward matches/base graph) against OV_reverse (detect_overlaps on the reverse matches/result graph), no enumeration, no ordering; reverse_fire -- thin orchestrator wiring these to footprint_is_consistent (existing) and the isomorphism check.

prior_candidates.statement: RECOMMENDED, answering 'is there any other way to say this set of created nodes corresponds to this match': for survived/id-preserving content -- YES, a free direct id-set comparison, no isomorphism needed; upgrade step 5 to do this (compare the SET of real ids a forward instance's survived nodes occupy against the candidate reverse match's, not just count(bindings)==1). For BORN content -- NO cheap shortcut found; do not push local anchored isomorphism into step 5 (avoids the 'multiple tries' combinatorial cost Sven flagged for compound cases with multiple candidate reverse matches per rule). Instead: leave born-node correspondence to step 9's existing global check, now fed more/better anchors by the strengthened step 5. This is close to Sven's own fallback (a simple activation count is easier and still useful) but sharper specifically where sharpness is free: id-preserving correspondence is not merely a count, it is a free set-equality check, so there is no reason to settle for a count THERE even though a count (or deferral) is the honest answer for born content.

derived_from.rule: apply the cheapest-available-and-sufficient check to each of the two structurally different cases separately, rather than using one uniform mechanism (count, or full local isomorphism) for both

goals: Answer Sven's design question precisely: does pushing per-match discrepancy comparison (recorded forward creations/survivals vs backward findings) into step 5 make step 9's global isomorphism check redundant? Is there a cheap way to say 'this set of created nodes corresponds to this match' besides a local anchored isomorphism check? Determine whether id-preserving vs genuinely-born content require fundamentally different answers.

prior_candidates.statement: collect_reversed_matches is extended to take forward apply_compound's result (output_maps, per-match) so it can compute, per instance i: forward_preserved[i] = set of real ids that are inherit-mapped for instance i's own operation (core.inherit keys mapped through output_maps[i]). For the reverse side, per instance i's single accepted binding (after the existing per_match_multiplicity==1 check), reverse_preserved[i] = set of real ids the reversed_op's OWN core.inherit maps through that binding. The check: forward_preserved[i] == reverse_preserved[i] EXACTLY (no more, no less, no different) -- compared per-instance only, never pooled or checked for disjointness against other instances (per pairing_already_exists_by_construction and overlap_source_already_computed, legitimate cross-instance overlap is expected and irrelevant to this per-instance equality check). SEPARATELY, a firing-count check: len(matches) == len(reversed_matches with exactly one binding each) -- already partially implied by the existing per_match_multiplicity==0/>1 checks, but explicitly named as the backstop for instances whose forward_preserved set is EMPTY (pure-born instances), where the id-set check alone cannot distinguish a correct empty-preserved match from a spurious extra one with no preserved ids.

derived_from.rule: implement the per-instance equality check directly using apply_compound's already-available output_maps and core.inherit, with no new cross-instance machinery needed

goals: Design the refined step 5: exact preserved-id-set comparison per matched pair (no more, no less, no different), plus a firing-count backstop to catch extra firings with empty preserved-id-sets (invisible to the set comparison since empty==empty trivially). Address Sven's caution: split/merge cases mean preserved-id-sets CAN legitimately overlap ACROSS different match instances (shared/redirected nodes, per Compound Match Resolution's own overlapping_nodes/boundary_crossings typology) -- the comparison must not assume cross-instance disjointness, only per-instance forward-vs-reverse equality.

prior_candidates.statement: Re-ran the exact real production case that exposed the original bug (add_init_00_single_single against real encoded 0+0=0). Result flipped from reversible:False to reversible:True. Verified this is NOT accidental by inspecting the underlying aggregation directly: forward born={20,21}/consumed={}/changed={6,7}; reverse born={}/consumed={20,21}/changed={6,7} -- an exact mirror duality (forward-born becomes reverse-consumed, changed stays changed in both directions). Node 7 (real prior structure) is now genuinely present in the reconstruction; node 21 (forward-only artifact) is genuinely absent. Single-rule synthetic regression test also still passes unchanged.

derived_from.rule: verify a fix against the exact real case that exposed the original bug, by inspecting the underlying mechanism directly rather than trusting the final boolean alone

goals: Fix build_reversed_graph2's construction defect: implement the born<->delete duality. Forward-born output nodes (new-input positions) must not feed a mapping edge into any new-output -- they represent pure creation-from-nothing, nothing to recover. Forward-deleted input nodes (new-output positions) must be forced into compile_core's output_only/born classification even when no natural mapping edge exists, since compile_core classifies purely by operational-edge presence and a position with zero incoming edges defaults to being misclassified as an input node.

imported_sven_refinements.statement: Sven (direct, this session): (1) check exact preserved-id-set equality per match -- no more, no less, no different. (2) additionally use a firing count to catch extra firings that have NO preserved id at all (invisible to the set check, since an empty set trivially equals another empty set). (3) beware split/merge ambiguity -- preserved-id-sets can legitimately overlap between DIFFERENT matches (shared/redirected nodes), not just within one match's own fan-in.

imported_pairing_already_exists_by_const.statement: Because build_reversed_operation constructs each reversed_op FROM a SPECIFIC forward instance's own binding (the physical-switch fix), the pairing between forward instance i and its reversed_op is already fixed by construction, not a search problem -- the id-set comparison is inherently per-instance (compare instance i's own forward-recorded preserved ids against instance i's own reverse-found preserved ids), not a cross-instance matching problem. The split/merge caution therefore does not require finding WHICH pairing is correct -- it requires the SET COMPARISON ITSELF to tolerate legitimate overlap with OTHER instances' sets, not assume disjointness.

derived_from.rule: the physical-switch fix already ties each reversed_op to one specific forward instance, so the harder 'which pairing' problem Sven flagged for born content does not apply to the survived/id-preserving comparison, which is already instance-scoped (imported, parents not re-grounded — external provenance)

imported_overlap_source_already_computed.statement: Which real nodes are legitimately shared between which match indices is ALREADY computed by detect_overlaps (overlapping_nodes: real_node -> set of match indices; boundary_crossings: (i,j) -> set of nodes) -- the same data step 6/resolve_reverse_compound already uses. The refined step 5 does not need to separately reason about split/merge in the abstract; it needs to NOT break when a real id legitimately appears in more than one instance's preserved-id-set, which is already a known, structurally-declared possibility, not an edge case to special-case from scratch.

imported_concrete_design.statement: collect_reversed_matches is extended to take forward apply_compound's result (output_maps, per-match) so it can compute, per instance i: forward_preserved[i] = set of real ids that are inherit-mapped for instance i's own operation (core.inherit keys mapped through output_maps[i]). For the reverse side, per instance i's single accepted binding (after the existing per_match_multiplicity==1 check), reverse_preserved[i] = set of real ids the reversed_op's OWN core.inherit maps through that binding. The check: forward_preserved[i] == reverse_preserved[i] EXACTLY (no more, no less, no different) -- compared per-instance only, never pooled or checked for disjointness against other instances (per pairing_already_exists_by_construction and overlap_source_already_computed, legitimate cross-instance overlap is expected and irrelevant to this per-instance equality check). SEPARATELY, a firing-count check: len(matches) == len(reversed_matches with exactly one binding each) -- already partially implied by the existing per_match_multiplicity==0/>1 checks, but explicitly named as the backstop for instances whose forward_preserved set is EMPTY (pure-born instances), where the id-set check alone cannot distinguish a correct empty-preserved match from a spurious extra one with no preserved ids.

derived_from.rule: implement the per-instance equality check directly using apply_compound's already-available output_maps and core.inherit, with no new cross-instance machinery needed (imported, parents not re-grounded — external provenance)

implemented_and_tested.statement: Implemented _preserved_ids_per_instance (exact per-instance forward-vs-reverse preserved-id-set equality, no cross-instance disjointness assumed) and a firing-count backstop (len(matches)==len(reversed_matches)), both wired into reverse_fire before the overlap/footprint/isomorphism steps. Verified: syntax OK, single-rule synthetic regression still passes (reversible:True, unchanged), and the real production case (add_init_00_single_single against real 0+0=0) STILL passes reversible:True under the new, stricter check -- confirming the earlier born<->inherit fix was not merely passing a weak test.

derived_from.rule: implement per the committed design and verify against both the existing synthetic regression and the real production case that exposed the original bug

chk_falsifier_implemented.method: does the id-set check risk being VACUOUSLY true for the real test case -- e.g. is forward_preserved actually nonempty and meaningfully checked, or did this instance have zero preserved ids (trivial empty==empty pass)?
chk_falsifier_implemented.result: Not vacuous: add_init_00_single_single's core.inherit has 9 entries (per earlier instrumentation this session: inherit maps 9 out_nodes to 9 distinct in_nodes), so forward_preserved for this instance is a real, nonempty 9-element set, and the check passing means all 9 real ids matched exactly between forward and reverse -- not an empty-set coincidence.

chk_relevance_implemented.method: connect the evidence to Sven's request
chk_relevance_implemented.result: Directly implements and verifies exact set comparison, the firing-count backstop, and the split/merge-tolerant per-instance scoping, against both synthetic and real cases.

committed.statement: Implemented _preserved_ids_per_instance (exact per-instance forward-vs-reverse preserved-id-set equality, no cross-instance disjointness assumed) and a firing-count backstop (len(matches)==len(reversed_matches)), both wired into reverse_fire before the overlap/footprint/isomorphism steps. Verified: syntax OK, single-rule synthetic regression still passes (reversible:True, unchanged), and the real production case (add_init_00_single_single against real 0+0=0) STILL passes reversible:True under the new, stricter check -- confirming the earlier born<->inherit fix was not merely passing a weak test.
